title: The Strait That Isn't: Iranian 'Shipping Management' Collapsed Real Traffic by 90%+ While Claiming Control
narrative:
Al Jazeera calls it 'managing shipping.' The data calls it a near-total collapse. Since February 2026, AIS satellite records show commercial transits through the Strait of Hormuz falling from approximately 175 daily passages to between 6 and 22 — a drop of 87 to 97 percent. Iranian officials insist commerce is flowing normally under their oversight. The UK's Maritime Trade Operations office describes something closer to silence: three confirmed commercial transits, period. The gap between the press release and the radar return is the story.

The Pentagon and State Department, for their part, have settled on 'the strait remains open' as their consistent line — while simultaneously deploying additional carrier groups through it. The verbal hedge and the operational reality are in direct tension. When your diplomatic posture and your naval posture say opposite things, one of them is not being honest about the policy objective.

What the AIS data cannot fully capture is the shadow. Sixty-two vessels have been identified as exhibiting AIS spoofing — transmitting false positions to disguise actual movements. The official traffic counts may be overstating the real number of vessels transiting, in either direction.
telemetry: AIS satellite data: Hormuz commercial traffic collapsed 87–97% since February 2026, from ~175 daily transits to 6–22. UK Maritime Trade Operations confirms 'extremely limited' movement with only 3 confirmed transits
62 vessels identified exhibiting AIS position spoofing — transmitting false coordinates to disguise actual movements through the strait
Insurance premiums for Hormuz transit reportedly spiked 400%+ as Lloyd's and other underwriters price in active combat risk
US Navy engaged Iranian tankers directly on May 8, 2026 — the kinetic event that terminated whatever remained of the 'orderly management' narrative
LinkedIn maritime analyst assessment: AIS data may be 'missing half the picture' — actual traffic disruption could be even more severe than satellite records show
analysis:
Tehran's 'shipping management' framing is doing two distinct jobs simultaneously, and poorly. Domestically, it signals strength — the Islamic Republic controls a chokepoint and the world must deal with it. Internationally, it provides diplomatic cover for the disruption while leaving room to deny intentionality. The problem is the AIS data doesn't care about the framing. When your claimed outcome (orderly commercial transit) and your measured outcome (near-zero traffic) diverge by 90 percent, you are not managing shipping. You are blocking it, with extra steps.

The US 'open strait' position has its own contradictions. Deploying carrier groups through a waterway you claim is functioning normally is either hedging or theater. If the strait were truly operating at acceptable capacity, the military posture would be deterrence-plus — keep the lanes open. Instead you have a carrier task force moving through waters where merchant vessels have dropped by 90 percent. That is a naval presence designed to signal, not to escort commerce.

The insurance market is doing what it always does: pricing physical reality. A 400-plus percent premium spike for a waterway that is nominally 'open' and 'managed' tells you what the private underwriting community actually believes about the risk. Lloyd's does not inflate premiums on political theater.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Iran's 'shipping management' and the US 'strait is open' claim are both underwritten by the same problem: the AIS data says traffic collapsed by 90-plus percent. The insurance market agrees. The naval deployments agree. At some point, the gap between the narrative and the quantitative record stops being spin and becomes its own kind of evidence.
